metadata.title: A Polynomial's Top Power Absorbs the Rest

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: A program's runtime is 3n squared plus 100n plus 500. Its competitor's is just 3n squared. On large inputs, they finish neck and neck. All that extra clutter just vanished. A two-step proof shows exactly why.

card.copy: A Polynomial's Top Power Absorbs the Rest

beats.narration_text: Big-O gives an upper bound on growth. T(n) is O(f(n)) means T(n) is at most C times f(n) for all n beyond some threshold n₀. For a polynomial, f(n) is its highest-degree term.

production_viz.label: Stacked bars for 3n² + 100n + 500 as n grows — n² bar dominates all others at large n

beats.narration_text: Prove that any degree-k polynomial is O(n^k) — and give the explicit constants C and n₀ that make the bound valid.

card.copy: T(n) = 3n² + 100n + 500. Prove this is O(n²). What are the explicit C and n₀ — and why is the proof only two steps?

beats.narration_text: Step 1: replace all terms with their absolute values. If any coefficient is negative, this can only increase the sum. T(n) is at most the sum of all |coefficient| times powers of n.

production_viz.label: Step 1: signs removed — |3| + |100| + |500| — absolute values can only increase the sum

beats.narration_text: Step 2: replace every lower power of n with n to the k — the highest degree. Each lower-power term is at most n to the k for n at least 1. The sum becomes at most the sum of all |coefficients| times n to the k.

production_viz.label: Step 2: all lower powers raised to n² — every term becomes at most |coeff| × n² for n ≥ 1

beats.narration_text: For T(n) equals 3n squared plus 100n plus 500: absolute values are 3, 100, 500. Sum equals 603. Conclusion: T(n) is at most 603 times n squared for all n at least 1. C equals 603, n₀ equals 1.

production_viz.label: Proof result: C = |3|+|100|+|500| = 603, n₀ = 1, T(n) ≤ 603n² for all n ≥ 1

beats.narration_text: At n equals 1: T(1) equals 603, and 603 times 1 equals 603. The bound is tight at n₀. At n equals 10: T(10) equals 1500, and 603 times 100 equals 60,300. Very loose — but valid.

production_viz.label: Verification: n=1: T(1)=603, bound=603 (tight); n=10: T(10)=1500, bound=60,300 (loose but valid)

beats.narration_text: The looseness doesn't matter. Big-O only requires the bound to hold, not be tight. What matters is that C and n₀ exist as finite constants. They clearly do.

production_viz.label: T(n) curve vs 603n² bound — gap grows at large n but bound always holds; looseness is irrelevant

beats.narration_text: Every polynomial of degree k — with any finite number of terms, any finite coefficients — is O(n to the k) by this two-step argument. The top power absorbs everything below it.

production_viz.label: General degree-k polynomial: C = Σ|aᵢ|, n₀ = 1 — top power absorbs all lower terms universally

beats.narration_text: Two-step Big-O proof for T(n) equals 3n squared plus 100n plus 500. Step 1 removes signs. Step 2 raises all powers to n squared. C equals 603, n₀ equals 1. T(n) is at most 603n squared for all n at least 1. Every degree-k polynomial is O(n to the k).

card.copy: Two-step proof: (1) |·| replaces signs, (2) raise all powers to n^k. C = Σ|aᵢ| = 603, n₀ = 1. T(n) ≤ 603n². Every degree-k polynomial is O(n^k).

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
